We, You Bunleng and Marcel Lemonde, Co-Investigating Judges of the Extraordinary
Chambers in the Courts ofCambodia,
Noting the Law on the establishment ofthe Extraordinary Chambers, dated 27 October
2004,
Noting Rules 29 and 55 ofthe Internal Rules ofthe Extraordinary Chambers,
Noting the judicial investigation opened against:
Kaing GuekEav alias Duch
Nuon Chea and others
Noting the request for protective measure’ subinitted by the Co-Prosecutors in the
Introductory Submission dated 18 July 2007 (paragraphs 119,120,121 and 125),
Noting our order of30 July 2007 concerning protective measures,
Noting the report by the Witness/Expert Support Section (WESU), dated 13 August 2007,
អង្គជំនុុំជម្រះវិសាមញ្ញក្នុងតុលាការកម្ពុជា មានទីតាំងស្លិតនៅ ផ្លូវជាតិលេខ៤ សង្កាត់ ចោមចៅ ខណ្ឌដង្កោ ក្រុង ភ្នំពេញ ប្រអប់សំបុត្រលេខ៧9 |

00170469
លេខ/ សុះ D12/VI
Noting our order of 24 August 2007, concerning the request for a copy ofthe case file,
filed by the defence the preceding day,
Noting the supplementary WESU report, dated 3 September 2007,
Noting the observations by Duch’s lawyers on the Co-Prosecutors’ request concerning
protective measures, accompanied by a request for a copy of the case file, dated 13
September 2007,
Noting the Co-Prosecutors’ response to these observations, dated 21 September 2007,
Noting the delegation ofpower for signature by Judge Marcel Lemonde, dated 5 October
2007
SUMMARY OF THE PROCEEDINGS AND REQUESTS BY THE PARTIES
1. By order dated 30 July 2007, the Co-Investigating Judges:
- partially rejected the request for protective measures submitted by the Co-
Prosecutors in the Introductory Submission,
- decided to prohibit, provisionally, the delivery of copies to the lawyers of the
parties,
- reiterated the prohibition of disclosure to the public of any information obtained
by consultation of the case file and particularly the names and the information
permitting the identification or location ofwitnesses,
- imposed, in the following terms, the conditions for admissibility of any new
request for protective measures: “in support of such request, they must specify:
(a) the identity ofthe witnesses, victims or third_persons who would benefit from
these measures; and (b) how, in the absence ofthe measures, the lives and health
of these witnesses, victims, their families or their kin, would be placed in grave
danger.”
2. The Co-Investigating Judges are seised ofa new filing, dated 17 August 2007’, in
which the Co-Prosecutors request us to:
(1) GRANT additional measures ofprotection to the victims and witnesses based
on the factual and legal submissions made above in addition to the measures
already granted in the Protective Measures Decision of30 July 2007.
(2) DIRECT that the names and identifying characteristics be redacted of all
witnesses and victims contained in the following documents:
' TheFrenchtranslation is notcommunicatedtotheOfficeofCUatthe date ofthis Order.
អង្គជំនុំជម្រុះវិសាមញ្ញក្នុងតុលាការកម្ពុជាមានទីតាំងស្ថិតនៅ ផ្គូវជាតិលេខ៤ សង្កាត់ ចោមចៅ ខណ្ឌ័ដង្កោ ក្រង ភ្នំពេញ ប្រអប់សំបុត្រលេខ៧១ 2

(b) the Introductory Submission and related footnotes;
(c) Annex B ofthe Introductory Submission (the witness list);
(d) AnnexC ofthe Introductory Submission (the document index) including
a complete redaction ofthe “author”, “full name” and “description” fields.
(3) ORDER the redaction of names and places of residence of those who gave
statements in Documents No. 19.1 to 19.230 filed along with the Introductory
Submission.
(4) ORDER that all the requested redactions be made. both in the hardcopy and
electronic versions of the material listed in (1) to (3) above including the
disclosed Casemap file from which Annexes B and C were produced.
(5) ORDER that the names and any other identifying information concerning all
the witnesses be kept in a separate register and be not included in any existing
or future record ofthese Extraordinary Chambers, unless otherwise ordered.
(6) ORDER that pseudonyms be designated for each protected witness that shall
be used pending athorough risk assessmentas requested herein. =
(7) ORDER that the name and other redacted information be disclosed to the
defence, the Charged Person and any other eligible person not less than 21
days before that witness is expected to appear for testimony for the first time
before a judicial organ ofthese Extraordinary Chambers.
(8) ORDER that the defence or the Charged Person shall not make an
អង្គជំនុំជម្រុះវិសាមញ្ញក្នុងតុលាការកម្ពុជា មានទីតាំងស្លិតនៅ ផ្លូវជាតិលេខ៤ សង្កាត់ចោមចៅ ខណ្ឌ័ ដង្កោ ក្រុង ភ្នំពេញ ប្រអប់សំបុត្រលេខ៧១ 3
ទូរស័ព្ទលេខ +៨៥៥(០)២៣ ២១៨៩៤១ ទូរសាលេខ +៨&៥៥(០)២៣ ២១៨៩៤១។

00170471
លេខ/ ស០: D12/VI
independent determination ofthe identity ofa protected witness or encourage
or otherwise aid any person to attempt to determine the identity of any such
person,
(9) ORDER that the names and any other identifying information concerning all
protected witnesses be communicated only to the Victims and Witnesses /
Experts Support Units by the Office of the Co-Investigating Judges or the
Office of the Co-Prosecutors in accordance with established procedure and
only in orderto implement protective measures forthose individuals.
(10) ORDER that the counsel for the defence, the Charged Person and any other
eligible person be granted access to the above-described material only after
such redaction is made.
(11) ORDER that pending a thorough assessment in respect of the threats to
individual victims and witnesses, these redactions must remain in place.
(12) CONSULT the Victims Unit and the Witnesses / Experts Support Unit before
ruling on this request.
3. In response to the request by Duch’s lawyers on 23 August 2007, the Co-
Investigating Judges have authorized the latter to consult the case file and obtain a
copy ofthe Introductory Submission in Khmer and French, with the exception of
the footnotes.
4. In their observations dated 13 September 2007, Duch’s lawyers claim that the Co-
Prosecutors’ request does not satisfy the level of precision required by the order
of 30 July 2007 and is partly unfounded, as regards the confidentiality of the
judicial investigation due to the fact that the defence already has knowledge ofthe
witnesses’ names. In consequence, they ask the Co-Investigating Judges to reject
the request and reiterate the right ofthe defence to obtain an unexpurgated copy
ofthe case file.
REASONS FOR THE DECISION
The Law
5. First and foremost, it is important to note that the Co-Prosecutors’ request intends
to accord the benefit of protective measures to persons who have not yet
participated in the proceedings and can be qualified as “potential witnesses”. Yet,
Rule 29 of the Internal Rules aims to guarantee “the protection of Victims who
participate in the proceedings, whether as complainants or Civil Parties, or
អង្គជំនុំជម្រះវិសាមញ្ញក្នុងតុលាការកម្ពុជា មានទីតាំងស្លិតនៅ ផ្តូវជាតិលេខ៤ សង្កាត់ ចោមចៅ ខណ្ឌ័ដង្កោ ក្រុង ភ្នំពេញ ប្រអប់សំបុត្រលេខ៧១ 4
ទូសើព្ឋលេខ +៨៥៥(០)២៣ ២១៨៩៤១ ទូរសាលេខ -៨៥៥(០)២៣ ២១៨៩៤១។
ExtraordinaryChambersintheCourtsofCambodia,NationalRoad4,ChoamChao,DangkoaPhnomPenh
MailPoBox71,PhnomPenhTel:+855(0)23218914Fax:+855(0)23218941.
Chambresextraordinairesauseindestribunauxcambodgiens,Routenationale4,ChoamChao,Dangkoa,PhnomPenh
Boitepostale71,PhnomPenh.Tel:+855(0)23218914Fax:+855(0)23218941.

00170472
លេខ/ ស: D12/VI
witnesses”. Consequently, the only persons who can be considered as witnesses at
this stage are those persons who have made statements during the preliminary
investigation conducted by the Co-Prosecutors or who have been interviewed
under rogatory letters of the Co-Investigating Judges. It is obvious that this
request goes much further on this point. As the WESU Unit has indicated,
however, it is only when actually hearing witnesses that the Co-Investigating
Judges are able to take into account any perception of danger or real risk. Until
now, no specific risk has been established for the interviewed persons.
In support oftheir request, the Co-Prosecutors raise a number oflegal arguments
founded on the case law of the ad hoc tribunals and the International Criminal
Court. From this case law, it appears that the practice of the ad hoc tribunals
concerning the protection of victims and witnesses varies widely depending on
the specific provisions of their statute, the local security situation and other
particular factors to each case.
Yet, beyond these apparent differences, there is a strong tendency in the
legislation and international practice that permits the identification of minimum
criteria concerning the protection of victims and witnesses: before these
international tribunals, except the Special Court for Sierra Leone, the granting of
generalised protective measures is wholly exceptional. Therefore, our order of30
July 2007 complies with the majority practice of the ad hoc tribunals when
allowing for protective measures in favour ofdesignated persons on the basis ofa
specified danger. The requestdoes notreach the required level ofspecificity since
it simply repeats the demand for general protection ofevery person mentioned in
the Introductory Submission and in the annexed documents until a full risk
analysis has been conducted, without saying how the absence of protective
measures will place the life or security ofeach ofthese persons in grave danger.
It is also important to stress that, since theprocedural system applicable beforethe
ECCC is very different from the system applicable before the international
tribunals, the scope of the case law ofthe ad hoc tribunals and the International
Criminal Court is necessarily limited.
Accordingly, the analysis by the ICTY Trial Chamber in the Blaskic case (“the
victims and witnesses merit protection, even from the accused, during the
preliminary proceedings and continuing until a reasonable time before the start
ofthe trial itself; from that timeforth, however, the right ofthe accused to an
equitable trial must takeprecedence’) cannot be purely and simply transposed to
the ECCC. The concept of the “start of the trial” after which the exercise of
defence rights must be guaranteed, is not identical here, the respect for the rights
ofthe defence being required from thebeginning ofthejudicial investigation.
2Prosecutorv. Blaskic, ICTY, Trial ChamberI,Judgment,3 March 2000, para. 50.
អង្គជំនុំជម្រះវិសាមញ្ញក្នុងតុលាការកម្ពុជា មានទីតាំងស្លិតនៅ ផ្លូវជាតិលេខ៤ សង្កាត់ ចោមចៅ ខណ្ឌ័ ដង្កោ ក្រង ភ្នំពេញ ប្រអប់សំបុត្រលេខ៧១9 5
ទូរស័ព្ទលេខ +៨៥៥(០)២៣ ២១៨៩៤១ ទូរសាលេខ -=៨៥៥(០)២៣២១៨៥៤១។
ExtraordinaryChambersintheCourtsofCambodia,NationalRoad4,ChoamChao,DangkoaPhnomPenh
MailPoBox71,PhnomPenhTel:+855(0)23218914Fax:+855(0)23218941.
Chambresextraordinairesauseindestribunauxcambodgiens,Routenationale4,ChoamChao,Dangkoa,PhnomPenh
Boitepostale71,PhnomPenh.Tel:+855(0)23 218914Fax:+855(0)23218941.

00170473
លេខ/ស០: D12/VI
10. Equally, a number of the protective measures that have been applied by the
international tribunals, and requested by the Co-Prosecutors, are totally unsuited
to the procedural system applicable before the ECCC. For example, it would be
paradoxical at the very least to order the disclosure of the witnesses’ identity to
the defence “not less than 21 days before” their appearance, because, in this
system, the Co-Investigating Judges only inform the defence of a witness
interview after his or her appearance.
The facts
11. In support of their request, the Co-Prosecutors develop a number of factual
arguments concerning security risks that justify the granting of protective
measures. They consider that thatthe Co-Investigating Judges have referred to the
still fragile context of Cambodian society and have made an analogy with the
situation in Sierra Leone.
12. While it is true that the Co-Investigating Judges reiterated, in their two detention
orders, that the context in Cambodia is still fragile, this was with reference to the
possible release of the charged persons. Since these persons are detained,
however, the need to adopt special protective measures for witnesses is evidently
less compelling.
13, On the second point, it should be noted that by sole reference to the objective
criteria applied by the United Nations for the establishment of security phases,
Cambodia is currently at level zero while Sierra Leone was at level 3 at the time
when proceedings began in that country. Furthermore, it is important to remember
that the complete surrender ofthe Khmer Rouge occurred in 1998, years before
the beginning of the ECCC proceedings, and that the crimes within the
jurisdiction ofthe court with respect to which the witnesses and victims could be
called to testify were committed more than thirty years ago.
14. The Co-Prosecutors strive to demonstrate the existence of fear and reluctance to
testify before the ECCC (and, more generally, before the Cambodian courts) by
referring to two studies by the Documentation Center of Cambodia (DC-CAM)
and the Centre for Social Development (CSD) respectively. Without questioning
the relevance ofthese studies, the Co-Investigating Judges note that the examples
quoted do not appear to be decisive because they concern very reduced samples.
Thus, the fact that “12 out of 23 interviewed persons were afraid to appear as a
witness”® cannot suffice to demonstrate the existence of risks for the security of
potential witnesses and victims in general. Similarly, the fact that one of these
3 Request, para. 24, citingរ Voice ofTakeo:Apilotfearassessmentwith respecttopossible
witnessesofthe ECCC, Documentation CenterofCambodia, October2006, p. 23.
អង្គជំនុុំជម្រះវិសាមញ្ញក្នុងតុលាការកម្ពុជា មានទីតាំងស្ថិតនៅ ផ្លូវជាតិលេខ៤ សង្កាត់ ចោមចៅ ខណ្ឌ័ដង្កោ ក្រុង ភ្នំពេញ ប្រអប់សំបុត្រលេខ៧9 6
ទូរស័ព្ទលេខ +៨៥៥(០)២៣ ១៨៨៤១ ទូរសាលេខ +៨៥៥(០)២៣ ២១៨៩៤១។
ExtraordinaryChambersintheCourtsofCambodia,NationalRoad4,ChoamChao,DangkoaPhnomPenh
MailPoBox71, PhnomPenhTel:+855(0)23218914Fax:+855(0)23218941.
Chambresextraordinairesauseindestribunauxcambodgiens,Routenationale4,ChoamChao,Dangkoa,PhnomPenh
Boitepostale71,PhnomPenh.Tel:+855(0)23 218914Fax:+855(0)23218941.

00170474
លេខ/ ០: D1i2/VI
persons declared that Duch had warned her, 30 years ago, not to reveal his
association with the S-21 structure cannot be qualified as a current danger in
2007.
15. It is important to stress thatthe WESU Unit has indicated that the Co-Prosecutors’
request does not totally reflect its own conclusions, which tend to show that a
certain number of witnesses were perfectly ready to testify publicly and that, for
the majority, the risk level was low. This impression is strongly supported by the
fact that some ofthe potential witnesses mentioned are already well known to the
public and to the charged persons, through their earlier public declarations in the
media, and that for those who have been interviewed by DC-CAM, their names
have already been made available to the whole world on internet. It can hardly be
asserted that to see these names appear in the case file as potential witnesses
creates a supplementary danger forthe persons mentioned.
10. In consideration ofthese elements, the Co-Investigating Judges can only state that
the new request by the Co-Prosecutors fails to specify precisely enough the
identity ofthe persons who should benefit from protective measures. Nor does it
give any evidence ofrisks that could place the persons mentioned in the case file
in grave danger, as required by our Order of 30 July 2007. This request, which
demands the implementation of protective measures of a general nature for the
benefit ofpersons who “may” appear before the ECCC, would considerably limit
the exercise ofdefence rights at this stage ofthe judicial proceedings. Thejudicial
investigation is confidential, which is protective but also respectful ofthe rights of
the defence.
17. Furthermore, considering the advice given by the Witness/Experts Support
Section, there is no reason to maintain the prohibition of copies of the case file
which had been decided provisionally. Indeed, under Rule 55(6) of the Internal
Rules, the charged person has the right to the necessary time and facilities to
prepare his defence and his lawyers must be able to access the case file and obtain
acopy ofit “at all times”.
18. Accordingly, the Prosecution request is unfounded and the request for a copy of
the case file by Duch’s lawyers is allowed, while still maintaining the terms ofour
order of 30 July 2007 concerning the prohibition to disclose any information
obtained through the consultation ofthe case file, and particularly the names and
any information permitting the identification or localization ofthe witnesses.
For these reasons
1. We reject the Co-Prosecutors’ additional request;
អង្គជំនុំជម្រុះវិសាមញ្ញក្នុងតុលាការកម្ពុជា មានទីតាំងស្លិតនៅ ផ្លូវជាតិលេខ៤ សង្កាត់ ចោមចៅ ខណ្ឌដង្កោ ក្រុង ភ្នំពេញ ប្រអប់សំបុត្រលេខ៧១ 7

00170475
smsa/No: D12/VI
2. We authorize Duch’s lawyers to obtain a copy ofthe case file;
3. We reiterate that everyone who has access to the case file is prohibited from
disclosing publicly, in any manner whatsoever, any information obtained through
consultation of the case file, especially the names and relevant information that
would allow the identification and location of the witness, under penalty of
disciplinary or criminal sanctions in accordance with Rules 35 and 38 of the
Internal Rules ofthe Extraordinary Chambers;
4, We state that, in the present order, the term “public” means any physical person or
legal entity, government, organization, entity, group and media, and that it
includes, in particular, the family, friends and relatives of the Charged Person;
moreover, the term “case file” and “annexed documents” means any information,
statements, minutes, reports, photos or other items submitted, whether in paper or
in electronic format.
The present order was written in Khmer and French, then translated into English.
Done in Phnom Penh, on 09 October 2007
សហរេចាក្រមេស៊ីបអរង្កេត
